evals.prompt: Audit the motion on our /pricing route — I want to know where it feels cheap and what to fix, in priority order.
evals.expected_output: A prioritized punch list (most-severe first) with exact files, severity, reasoning, and concrete fixes across the audit dimensions (token consistency, physicality, performance, reduced-motion, accessibility, hallmark gaps); it diagnoses and routes implementation to the owning skill rather than rewriting the code itself.
assertions: Returns a prioritized punch list (most-severe first) with file references, severity, and concrete fixes
Checks design-token consistency (hardcoded durations/easings vs tokenized values, intent naming)
Flags missing reduced-motion branches for camera moves, parallax, loops, and bounce
Identifies missing hallmark-motion opportunities (or explicitly notes there are none) rather than only listing defects
Routes the actual implementation fixes to the owning skill (design-motion-system / expo-motion / web-three-r3f / r3f-scene-polish / gsap) rather than building them itself
Treats any static-analyzer output as leads to verify against the real code, not final findings

evals.prompt: Verify our app respects prefers-reduced-motion everywhere — find every animation that ignores it.
evals.expected_output: Reviews each motion source (camera moves, parallax, idle loops, bounce, entrances) for a reduced-motion branch that preserves functional feedback, lists offenders by file and severity, and notes that state must stay clear when large motion is removed — across both web and native surfaces.
assertions: Enumerates motion sources (camera travel, parallax, idle loops, bounce, entrances) and checks each for a reduced-motion branch
Confirms the reduced-motion path preserves functional feedback / keeps state clear rather than only disabling animation
Reports offenders as a punch list with file references and severity
Covers both web (prefers-reduced-motion) and native (useReducedMotion / ReduceMotion.System) surfaces
Diagnoses only and routes the fixes to the owning per-stack skill instead of implementing them

evals.prompt: Review my React Three Fiber scene for performance risks before we ship — draw calls, frame loop, that kind of thing.
evals.expected_output: Flags setState in useFrame, per-frame allocations, missing delta-time, disposal gaps, and DPR/shadow/postprocessing budget with file references and severity as a punch list; diagnoses only and routes correctness fixes to web-three-r3f and look-dev fixes to r3f-scene-polish.
assertions: Flags setState-in-useFrame, per-frame allocations, and missing delta-time normalization in the frame loop
Checks disposal, DPR, shadow, and postprocessing budget for frame-rate / draw-call risk
Returns findings as a prioritized punch list with file references and severity
Routes correctness/setup fixes to web-three-r3f and cinematic look-dev fixes to r3f-scene-polish rather than implementing them
Verifies analyzer leads against the actual code before reporting

evals.prompt: Does our onboarding flow actually feel premium, or generic? Critique it and tell me where a signature motion would help.
evals.expected_output: Judges the surface against motion quality gates, calls out where motion feels generic/templated, identifies specific missing hallmark-motion opportunities, assesses interaction physicality, and returns a prioritized list — without building the animations.
assertions: Judges the surface against motion quality gates and calls out where it feels generic / templated
Identifies specific missing hallmark-motion opportunities where a signature interaction would add value
Assesses interaction physicality (velocity-aware release, interruptibility, cancellation-safety)
Returns a prioritized punch list rather than implementing the animations
Routes any build work to design-motion-system or the owning per-stack skill
